title: The Magicians of Caprona — Chapter 3 (Fragment 3)
sourceText:
The boys were longing to watch the cardboard figures being brought to life, but they were snatched indoors by their mother. Elizabeth’s hair was soaking from her shift on the bridge and she was yawning until her jaw creaked, but this did not prevent her doing a very thorough scrubbing, combing and dressing job on Paolo and Tonino. By the time they came down into the yard again, each with his hair scraped wet to his head and wearing uncomfortable broad white collars above their stiff Eton jackets, the spell was done. The spell-streamers had been carefully wound into the harness, and the coachman clothed in a paper coat covered with spells on the inside. Four glossy white horses were stamping as they were backed into their traces. The coachman was sitting on the box adjusting his leaf-green hat.

“Splendid!” said Old Niccolo, bustling out. He looked approvingly from the boys to the coach. “Get in, boys. Get in, Domenico. We have to pick up Umberto from the University.”

Tonino said good-bye to Benvenuto and climbed into the coach. It smelt of mold, in spite of the dusting. He was glad his grandfather was so cheerful. In fact everyone seemed to be. The family cheered as the coach rumbled to the gateway, and Old Niccolo smiled and waved back. Perhaps, Tonino thought, something good was going to come from this visit to the Duke, and no one would be so worried after this.

The journey in the coach was splendid. Tonino had never felt so grand before. The coach rumbled and swayed. The hooves of the horses clattered over the cobbles just as if they were real, and people hurried respectfully out of their way. The coachman was as good as spells could make him. Though puddles dimpled along every street, the coach was hardly splashed when they drew up at the University, with loud shouts of “Whoa there!”

Uncle Umberto climbed in, wearing his red and gold Master’s gown, as cheerful as Old Niccolo. “Morning, Tonino,” he said to Paolo. “How’s your cat? Morning,” he said to Domenico. “I hear the Petrocchis beat you up.” Domenico, who would have died sooner than insult even a Petrocchi, went redder than Uncle Umberto’s gown and swallowed noisily. But Uncle Umberto never could remember which younger Montana was which. He was too learned. He looked at Tonino as if he was wondering who he was, and turned to Old Niccolo. “The Petrocchis are sure to help,” he said. “I had word from Chrestomanci.”

“So did I,” said Old Niccolo, but he sounded dubious.

The coach rumbled down the rainswept Corso and turned out across the New Bridge, where it rumbled even more loudly. Paolo and Tonino stared out of the rainy windows, too excited to speak. Beyond the swollen river, they clopped uphill, where cypresses bent and lashed in front of rich villas, and then among blurred old walls. Finally they rumbled under a great archway and made a crisp turn around the gigantic forecourt of the Palace. In front of their own coach, another coach, looking like a toy under the huge marble front of the Palace, was just drawing up by the enormous marble porch. This carriage was black too, with crimson shields on its doors, in which ramped black leopards. They were too late to see the people getting out of it, but they gazed with irritated envy at the coach itself and the horses. The horses were black, beautiful slender creatures with arched necks.

“I think they’re real horses,” Paolo whispered to Tonino.

Tonino had no time to answer, because two footmen and a soldier sprang to open the carriage door and usher them down, and Paolo jumped down first. But after him, Old Niccolo and Uncle Umberto were rather slow getting down. Tonino had time to look out of the further window at the Petrocchi carriage moving away. As it turned, he distinctly saw the small crimson flutter of a spell-streamer under the harness of the nearest black horse. So there! Tonino thought triumphantly. But he rather thought the Petrocchi coachman was real. He was a pale young man with reddish hair which did not match his cherry-colored livery, and he had an intent, concentrating look, as if it was not easy driving those unreal horses. That look was too human for a cardboard man.

When Tonino finally climbed down on Domenico’s nervous heels, he glanced up at their own coachman for comparison. He was efficient and jaunty. He touched a stiff hand to his green hat and stared straight ahead. No, the Petrocchi coachman was real all right, Tonino thought enviously.

words.definition: A strong desire or craving for something.
words.example: The boys were longing to watch the cardboard figures being brought to life.
words.exampleText: The boys were longing to watch the cardboard figures being brought to life.

words.definition: To take something or someone quickly or suddenly away.
words.example: They were snatched indoors by their mother.
words.exampleText: They were snatched indoors by their mother.

words.definition: Extremely wet.
words.example: Elizabeth’s hair was soaking from her shift on the bridge.
words.exampleText: Elizabeth’s hair was soaking from her shift on the bridge.

words.definition: Opening the mouth wide and inhaling deeply because of tiredness or boredom.
words.example: She was yawning until her jaw creaked.
words.exampleText: She was yawning until her jaw creaked.

words.definition: To make a long high sound when something moves or is moved.
words.example: Her jaw creaked as she yawned.
words.exampleText: Her jaw creaked as she yawned.

words.definition: Complete and careful; with great attention to detail.
words.example: She did a very thorough scrubbing, combing and dressing job.
words.exampleText: She did a very thorough scrubbing, combing and dressing job.

words.definition: The act of cleaning something by rubbing hard, often with a brush or cloth.
words.example: A thorough scrubbing, combing and dressing job was done on Paolo and Tonino.
words.exampleText: A thorough scrubbing, combing and dressing job was done on Paolo and Tonino.

words.definition: Not easily bent or changed; rigid.
words.example: They were wearing uncomfortable broad white collars above their stiff Eton jackets.
words.exampleText: They were wearing uncomfortable broad white collars above their stiff Eton jackets.

words.definition: A form of words used as a magical charm or incantation.
words.example: The spell was done, and the spell-streamers had been carefully wound into the harness.
words.exampleText: The spell was done, and the spell-streamers had been carefully wound into the harness.

words.definition: To wrap or twist something around an object.
words.example: The spell-streamers had been carefully wound into the harness.
words.exampleText: The spell-streamers had been carefully wound into the harness.

words.definition: A set of straps and fittings by which a horse or other draft animal is fastened to a cart or carriage.
words.example: The spell-streamers were wound into the harness.
words.exampleText: The spell-streamers were wound into the harness.

words.definition: Dressed or covered with clothing or a material.
words.example: The coachman clothed in a paper coat covered with spells on the inside.
words.exampleText: The coachman clothed in a paper coat covered with spells on the inside.

words.definition: Shiny and smooth in appearance.
words.example: Four glossy white horses were stamping as they were backed into their traces.
words.exampleText: Four glossy white horses were stamping as they were backed into their traces.

words.definition: To bring the foot down heavily and noisily on the ground.
words.example: The horses were stamping as they were backed into their traces.
words.exampleText: The horses were stamping as they were backed into their traces.

words.definition: To change something slightly to make it more suitable or effective.
words.example: The coachman was sitting on the box adjusting his leaf-green hat.
words.exampleText: The coachman was sitting on the box adjusting his leaf-green hat.

words.definition: Very good, impressive, or magnificent.
words.example: The journey in the coach was splendid.
words.exampleText: The journey in the coach was splendid.

words.definition: In a way that shows admiration or agreement.
words.example: Old Niccolo looked approvingly from the boys to the coach.
words.exampleText: Old Niccolo looked approvingly from the boys to the coach.

words.definition: Noticeably happy and optimistic.
words.example: His grandfather was so cheerful.
words.exampleText: His grandfather was so cheerful.

words.definition: Hesitating or doubting; not to be relied on.
words.example: Old Niccolo sounded dubious about the help from Chrestomanci.
words.exampleText: Old Niccolo sounded dubious about the help from Chrestomanci.

words.definition: Made a continuous deep, resonant sound.
words.example: The coach rumbled and swayed.
words.exampleText: The coach rumbled and swayed.

words.definition: To move slowly or rhythmically backwards and forwards or from side to side.
words.example: The coach rumbled and swayed as it moved.
words.exampleText: The coach rumbled and swayed as it moved.

words.definition: To make a continuous rattling sound as hard objects strike each other.
words.example: The hooves of the horses clattered over the cobbles.
words.exampleText: The hooves of the horses clattered over the cobbles.

words.definition: In a way that shows respect.
words.example: People hurried respectfully out of their way.
words.exampleText: People hurried respectfully out of their way.

words.definition: Showing earnest and eager attention.
words.example: The coachman had an intent, concentrating look.
words.exampleText: The coachman had an intent, concentrating look.

words.definition: Working in a well-organized and competent way without wasting time or resources.
words.example: Their own coachman was efficient and jaunty.
words.exampleText: Their own coachman was efficient and jaunty.

words.definition: Having or expressing a lively, cheerful, and self-confident manner.
words.example: Their own coachman was efficient and jaunty.
words.exampleText: Their own coachman was efficient and jaunty.
